elements.sent-2411-001: This paper presents a comprehensive study on aspect-ratio dependent optimization for specific on-resistance of three-dimensional high-k superjunction MOSFETs.
elements.sent-2411-002: The research introduces a Taylor modeling method, overcoming the computational limitations of the Bessel method.
elements.sent-2411-003: It also employs the Chynoweth model for more accurate breakdown voltage determination.
elements.sent-2411-004: The study provides a comparative analysis of four different superjunction structures, across five aspects: electric field, impact ionization integral, aspect ratio dependent optimization, charge imbalance effect and temperature.
elements.sent-2411-005: The findings offer valuable insights for the manufacturing guidance of superjunction structure selection.
elements.sent-2411-006: In the field of power devices, the breakdown voltage (BV) and the specific on-resistance (R$_{\rm on,sp}$) are the two critical parameters for assessing the quality of a device.
elements.sent-2411-007: However, it has been discovered that BV and R$_{\rm on,sp}$ are interdependent, a constraint known as the 'Silicon Limit'.
elements.sent-2411-008: The introduction of the Superjunction (SJ) structure has greatly alleviated this constraint\cite{cxb}\cite{siliconlimit}\cite{napoli}\cite{cxb1998}\cite{chen2002 , allowing devices to achieve higher BV and lower R$_{\rm on,sp}$ through the lateral steering of electric field lines (E-field lines).}
elements.sent-2411-009: Due to the charge imbalance and pronounced JFET effect in conventional SJs (C-SJ)\cite{hhm202203}\cite{3DJFET}\cite{zwt , the High-$k$ material (H$k$) has been proposed to replace the P-pillar to form the high-$k$ superjunction (H$k$-SJ)\cite{2013}\cite{methodology}\cite{EFL}\cite{HK .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Beyond this, driven by the need for process optimization and further performance enhancement, both H$k$-SJs and C-SJs are evolving towards a three dimensional (3D) direction \cite{hhm202203}\cite{3DJFET}\cite{zwt}\cite{2013}\cite{3D .}
elements.sent-2411-011: However, compared to the research on 3D C-SJs, the research on 3D H$k$-SJs is scarce and not in-depth.
elements.sent-2411-012: Generally speaking, the 3D H$k$-SJ is divided into two categories\cite{2013 , as shown in Fig. \ref{structure1 (a) and (b), namely 3DH$k$case1 and 3DH$k$case2, which differ in the position of the H$k$ layer and the lateral steering of E-field are also shown.}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \subfigure[]{ \includegraphics[scale=0.3]{fig1a.png} } \subfigure[]{ \includegraphics[scale=0.36]{fig1b.png} } \caption{Structure and E-field lines of (a) 3DH$k$case1 and (b) 3DH$k$case2. The E-field lines are calculated and drawn by MATLAB using the methodology in \cite{EFL}.} \label{structure1}
elements.sent-2411-013: Through literature review, the cutting-edge 3D H$k$-SJ research still has the following shortcomings: 1.
elements.sent-2411-014: The E-field modeling of 3D H$k$-SJs is limited to the Bessel method\cite{2013 , which is computationally intensive and time-consuming.. 2.}
elements.sent-2411-015: The optimization of 3D H$k$-SJs is not comprehensive, with no optimization dependent on the aspect ratio.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-016: Only the Fulop impact ionization integral model is used\cite{2013 , with no application of the Chynoweth precise model, leading to inaccurate BV \cite{hhm202203 .. 4.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: The research on 3D H$k$-SJs is relatively isolated, with no comparative studies between 2D H$k$-SJs (2DH$k$) and different types of 3D H$k$-SJs, resulting in poor manufacturing guidance.
elements.sent-2411-018: Considering the aforementioned reasons, this article proposes a Taylor modeling method for 3DH$k$case2 in Section II and uses the Taylor method and Chynoweth model to complete the aspect ratio dependent optimization.
elements.sent-2411-019: Section III will consider a comprehensive comparison and analysis of the advantages and disadvantages of 3D C-SJ, 2DH$k$, 3DH$k$case1, and 3DH$k$case2, comparing from five aspects: E-field, impact ionization integral, aspect ratio dependent optimization, charge imbalance, and temperature.
elements.sent-2411-020: Finally, in Section IV, we will place the 3D H$k$-SJ structure within MOSFETs and complete a comparative analysis of electrical characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-021: Section V will summarize the entire article.
elements.sent-2411-022: Before presenting the Taylor modeling approach, we first correct the error in the potential distribution expression ($V_{\mathrm{H k}$) in the H$k$ region of 3DH$k$case2 from \cite{2013 .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: First, the Poisson equation in 3D cylindrical coordinates is given by.
elements.figure-2411-002: \subfigure[]{\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{fig2a.png} } \subfigure[]{\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{efield1.png} } \subfigure[]{\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{error.png} } \caption{(a) The comparison between the calculation using equation (4) and simulation result along $F_{2}E_{2}$, with 3D depiction of entire E-field distribution under same condition. (b) The comparison between Bessel\cite{2013}, Taylor method and simulation under different a/b at r=b. (c) The error of Taylor method along $A_{2}$$B_{2}$ at r = b under different conditions. Error= abs(100\%×[E(Taylor)-E(Bessel)]/E(Bessel)).} \label{Taylor}
elements.sent-2411-024: \begin{equation}\begin{cases} \nabla^2 V_{\rm S}(r,z,\theta)=-\frac{q N}{\epsilon_{ \rm S}}, \nabla^2 V_{\mathrm{H}k}(r,z,\theta)=0. \end{cases}\end{equation} The equations above can be expanded and simplified as followes due to the symmetry, \begin{equation}\begin{cases} \frac{\partial^{2}V_{\rm S}}{\partial r^{2}}+(\frac{\partial V_{\rm S}}{\partial r})\frac{1}{r}+\frac{\partial^{2}V_{\rm S}}{\partial z^{2}}=-\frac{q N}{\epsilon_{\rm S}} , \frac{\partial^{2}V_{\mathrm{H}k}}{\partial r^{2}}+(\frac{\partial V_{\mathrm{H}k}}{\partial r})\frac{1}{r}+\frac{\partial^{2}V_{\mathrm{H}k}}{\partial z^{2}}=0 . \end{cases}\end{equation} For 3DH$k$case2, the boundary conditions can be written as \begin{equation}\begin{cases} V_{\rm S}\left( z=0 \right)=V_{\mathrm{H}k}\left( z=0 \right)=0, V_{\rm S}\left( z=W \right)=V_{\mathrm{H}k}\left( z=W \right)=-V_{\rm ap}, \epsilon_{\rm S}\left.\frac{\partial V_{\rm S}}{\partial r}\right|_{r=a}=\epsilon_{\mathrm{H}k}\left.\frac{\partial V_{\mathrm{H}k}}{\partial r}\right|_{r=a}, V_{\rm S}\left(r=a\right)=V_{\mathrm{H}k}\left(r=a\right), \left.\frac{\partial V_{\rm S}}{\partial r}\right|_{r=b}=\left.\frac{\partial V_{\mathrm{H}k}}{\partial r}\right|_{r=0}=0, \end{cases}\end{equation} where, $V_{\rm ap}$ means the applied voltage as shown in Fig.\ref{structure1 .}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation} \frac{\partial^{2}V_{\rm S}}{\partial r^{2}}+(\frac{\partial V_{\rm S}}{\partial r})\frac{1}{r}+\frac{\partial^{2}V_{\rm S}}{\partial z^{2}}=-\frac{q N}{\epsilon_{\rm S}} , \frac{\partial^{2}V_{Hk}}{\partial r^{2}}+(\frac{\partial V_{Hk}}{\partial r})\frac{1}{r}+\frac{\partial^{2}V_{Hk}}{\partial z^{2}}=0 . \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation} V_{\rm S}\left( z=0 \right)=V_{Hk}\left( z=0 \right)=0, V_{\rm S}\left( z=W \right)=V_{Hk}\left( z=W \right)=-V_{\rm ap}, \epsilon_{\rm S}\left.\frac{\partial V_{\rm S}}{\partial r}\right|_{r=a}=\epsilon_{Hk}\left.\frac{\partial V_{Hk}}{\partial r}\right|_{r=a}, V_{\rm S}\left(r=a\right)=V_{Hk}\left(r=a\right), \left.\frac{\partial V_{\rm S}}{\partial r}\right|_{r=b}=\left.\frac{\partial V_{Hk}}{\partial r}\right|_{r=0}=0, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-025: Finally, we can solve the potential distribution of H$k$ region from equations (2) and (3): \begin{equation}V_{\mathrm{H}k}=-\frac{V_{\rm ap}}{W}z+\frac{4NW^{2}q}{\pi^{3}\varepsilon_{\mathrm{H}k}}\cdot\sum_{n= \rm odd}\frac{I_{0}(\frac{n\pi r}{W})\sin(\frac{n\pi z}{W})}{n^{3}[\frac{\varepsilon_{\rm S}}{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{H}k}}I_{0}(\frac{n\pi a}{W})-\frac{\beta_{\rm n}}{\alpha_{\rm n}}I_{1}(\frac{n\pi a}{W})]}\end{equation} where, $\alpha_{\rm n =I_{1 (\frac{n\pi a {W )K_{1 (\frac{n\pi b {W )-I_{1 (\frac{n\pi b {W )K_{1 (\frac{n\pi a {W )$ and $\beta_{\rm n =I_{0 (\frac{n\pi a {W ) K_{1 (\frac{n\pi b {W )+I_{1 (\frac{n\pi b {W )K_{0 (\frac{n\pi a {W )$, respectively.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}V_{Hk}=-\frac{V_{\rm ap}}{W}z+\frac{4NW^{2}q}{\pi^{3}\varepsilon_{Hk}}\cdot\sum_{n= \rm odd}\frac{I_{0}(\frac{n\pi r}{W})\sin(\frac{n\pi z}{W})}{n^{3}[\frac{\varepsilon_{\rm S}}{\varepsilon_{Hk}}I_{0}(\frac{n\pi a}{W})-\frac{\beta_{\rm n}}{\alpha_{\rm n}}I_{1}(\frac{n\pi a}{W})]}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-026: Fig. \ref{Taylor (a) shows a comparison between the results calculated from equation (4) and the MEDICI (TCAD software) simulation results.}
elements.sent-2411-027: The excellent agreement between the two confirms the accuracy of equation (4).
elements.sent-2411-028: For the Taylor modeling method, we also use equation (2) and the boundary conditions in equation (3).
elements.sent-2411-029: Additionally, we perform a Taylor expansion of $V_{\rm S (r,z)$ and $V_{\mathrm{H k (r,z)$ at $r$ = b and $r$ = 0, respectively, retaining only the first three terms of the expansion.}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: They are found as: \begin{equation}\begin{cases}V_{\rm S}(r,z)=V_{\rm S}(b,z) +\left.\frac{\partial V_{\rm S}(b,z)}{\partial r}\right|_{r=b}\cdot(r-b)\\\quad\quad\quad\quad +\left.\frac{\partial^{2}V_{\rm S}(b,z)}{\partial r^{2}}\right|_{r=b}\cdot\frac{(r-b)^{2}}{2}, V_{\mathrm{H}k}(r,z)=V_{\mathrm{H}k}(0,z)+\left.\frac{\partial V_{\mathrm{H}k}(0,z)}{\partial r}\right|_{r=0}\cdot r\\\quad\quad\quad\quad +\left.\frac{\partial^{2}V_{\mathrm{H}k}(0,z)}{\partial r^{2}}\right|_{r=0}\cdot\frac{r^{2}}{2}.\end{cases}\end{equation} Then, through the calculation, $V_{\rm S (b,z)$ can be found as \begin{equation}V_{\rm S}(b,z)=(\frac{2}{\lambda^{2} T_{\rm d}^{2}}-1+\frac{b^{2}}{{2}T_{\rm d}^{2}})[A\exp(\lambda z)+B\exp(-\lambda z)]\nonumber -\frac{T_{\rm e}^{2}}{T_{\rm d}^{2}} \frac{q N}{\epsilon_{\rm S}} z^{2}+Dz+E,\end{equation} where $T_{\rm c}$, $T_{\rm d}$, and $T_{\rm e}$ are dimensioned constants with units of $\mu$m, $T_{\rm c ^{2 =-\frac{1 {2}\left[\frac{\epsilon_{\rm S {\epsilon_{\mathrm{H k a (a-b)-1.42(a-b)^{2}\right]$, $T_{\rm d ^{2 =\frac{1 {2}\left[a^{2 -\frac{\epsilon_{\rm S {\epsilon_{\mathrm{H k a (a-b)\right]$, and $\frac{1 {T_{\rm e ^{2 =\frac{2 {T_{\rm d ^{2 +\frac{1 {T_{\rm c ^{2 }$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}V_{\rm S}(r,z)=V_{\rm S}(b,z) +\left.\frac{\partial V_{\rm S}(b,z)}{\partial r}\right|_{r=b}\cdot(r-b)\\\quad\quad\quad\quad +\left.\frac{\partial^{2}V_{\rm S}(b,z)}{\partial r^{2}}\right|_{r=b}\cdot\frac{(r-b)^{2}}{2}, V_{Hk}(r,z)=V_{Hk}(0,z)+\left.\frac{\partial V_{Hk}(0,z)}{\partial r}\right|_{r=0}\cdot r\\\quad\quad\quad\quad +\left.\frac{\partial^{2}V_{Hk}(0,z)}{\partial r^{2}}\right|_{r=0}\cdot\frac{r^{2}}{2}.\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}V_{\rm S}(b,z)=(\frac{2}{\lambda^{2} T_{\rm d}^{2}}-1+\frac{b^{2}}{{2}T_{\rm d}^{2}})[A\exp(\lambda z)+B\exp(-\lambda z)]\nonumber -\frac{T_{\rm e}^{2}}{T_{\rm d}^{2}} \frac{q N}{\epsilon_{\rm S}} z^{2}+Dz+E,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-031: Above three are structural constants which only depend on the structure parameters such as a and b.
elements.sent-2411-032: The coefficients $A$, $B$, $D$ and $E$ are found as \begin{equation}\left\{\begin{aligned}A = \frac{T_{\rm e}^2 q N / \epsilon_{\rm S}}{\exp(W\lambda) + 1}, B = \frac{T_{\rm e}^2 q N / \epsilon_{\rm S} \cdot \exp(W\lambda)}{\exp(W\lambda) + 1}, D = -\frac{T_{\rm d}^2 V_{\rm ap} - T_{\rm e}^2 q N W^2 / \epsilon_{\rm S}}{T_{\rm d}^2 W}, E = -\frac{T_{\rm e}^2 q N / \epsilon_{\rm S} \cdot \left(-2T_{\rm d}^2 \lambda^2 + b^2 \lambda^2 + 4\right)}{2T_{\rm d}^2 \lambda^2}.\end{aligned}\right.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\left\{A = \frac{T_{\rm e}^2 q N / \epsilon_{\rm S}}{\exp(W\lambda) + 1}, B = \frac{T_{\rm e}^2 q N / \epsilon_{\rm S} \cdot \exp(W\lambda)}{\exp(W\lambda) + 1}, D = -\frac{T_{\rm d}^2 V_{\rm ap} - T_{\rm e}^2 q N W^2 / \epsilon_{\rm S}}{T_{\rm d}^2 W}, E = -\frac{T_{\rm e}^2 q N / \epsilon_{\rm S} \cdot \left(-2T_{\rm d}^2 \lambda^2 + b^2 \lambda^2 + 4\right)}{2T_{\rm d}^2 \lambda^2}.\right.}\end{equation}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.4\linewidth]{TRop.png} \includegraphics[width=0.4\linewidth]{fig3b.png} \includegraphics[width=0.4\linewidth]{fig3c.png} \includegraphics[width=0.4\linewidth]{BVRon.png} \caption{Aspect ratio dependent optimization results (Taylor method) of (a) $R_{\rm on,sp}$, (b) $N$ and (c) $W$ under the condition of 3DH$k$case2 with a=2 {$\mu$}m and K varies from 20 to 100. (d) $R_{\rm on,sp}$-BV curve used for optimization accuracy confirmation. } \label{OPT1}
elements.sent-2411-033: This is because, as stated in \cite{2013 and verified through simulations, avalanche breakdown occurs first along the E-field line from $A_{2}$ to $B_{2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The results obtained using the Taylor method, the Bessel method\cite{2013 and simulation demonstrate good consistency with each other.}
elements.sent-2411-035: The error of Taylor method is depicted in Fig. \ref{Taylor (c) which indicates that with different doping concentration (N), $V_{\rm ap}$ and K (K = $\varepsilon_{\rm S / \varepsilon_{\mathrm{H k}$), the error along the z-direction at r = b mainly less than 2.5\%.}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: The error at z = 0 is relatively large, but this does not affect the accuracy of subsequent calculations because z = 0 corresponds to the point where the E-field reaches its minimum value ( as shown in Fig. \ref{Taylor (b)).}
elements.sent-2411-037: Since the E-field is minimal at this location, it does not influence the accuracy of the subsequent impact ionization integral calculation.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Aspect Ratio Dependent Optimization using Taylor Method
elements.sent-2411-038: For superjunction devices, there are many optimization methods available.
elements.sent-2411-039: Unlike in \cite{2013 , here we select the critical depletion and critical breakdown as two constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Following the methodology outlined in \cite{methodology , we can perform an R$_{\rm on,sp}$ optimization that depends on the aspect ratio (AR$_{\rm S}$, AR$_{\rm S}$ = W/ [2×(Width of N region)]).}
elements.sent-2411-041: R$_{\rm on,sp}$ for two cases can be determined as \begin{equation}R_{\rm on,sp}=\begin{cases}\frac{W}{q \mu_{\rm n} N}\cdot\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}} , {\mathrm{for\; 3DH}k \mathrm{case1}}, \frac{W}{q \mu_{\rm n} N}\cdot\frac{b^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}} , {\mathrm{for\; 3DH}k \mathrm{case2}}. \end{cases}\end{equation} { The breakdown conditions can be determined through the calculation of impact ionization integral.}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}R_{\rm on,sp}=\frac{W}{q \mu_{\rm n} N}\cdot\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}} , {for\; 3DHk case1}, \frac{W}{q \mu_{\rm n} N}\cdot\frac{b^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}} , {for\; 3DHk case2}. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-042: The Chynoweth model \cite{chynoweth1958ionization is appiled for higher accuracy of confirming breakdown, \begin{equation}I_{\rm n}=\int \alpha_{\rm n} \, \exp \left ( {\int^s (\alpha_{\rm p} -\alpha_{\rm n}) ds'} \right ) ds. \label{in}\end{equation} \par Using the methodology from \cite{methodology and MATLAB calculations, the optimization results for R$_{\rm on,sp (opt)}$, N$_{\rm (opt)}$ and W$_{\rm (opt)}$ are presented in Fig. \ref{OPT1 (a), (b) and (c), respectively, for given values of BV (800 V and 1000 V).}}}
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}I_{\rm n}=\int \alpha_{\rm n} \, \exp \left ( {\int^s (\alpha_{\rm p} -\alpha_{\rm n}) ds'} \right ) ds. \label{in}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-043: From the three figures, it can be observed that under the two constraints mentioned before and given BV, as AR$_{\rm S}$ increases, the doping concentration N$_{\rm (opt)}$ gradually increases while the device height W$_{\rm (opt)}$ decreases, resulting in a reduction in R$_{\rm on,sp (opt)}$.
elements.sent-2411-044: This implies that, in the manufacturing process, one can achieve a lower R$_{\rm on,sp (opt)}$ by increasing the AR$_{\rm S}$ (or, say, reducing the width of the N-region).
elements.sent-2411-045: Additionally, using high dielectric constant materials as high-$k$ material and reducing the BV can further reduce R$_{\rm on,sp (opt)}$.
elements.sent-2411-046: Specifically, the minimum value, 4.156 $m\Omega \cdot cm^{2}$, can be achieved when BV = 800 V, K = 100, AR$_{\rm S}$ = 70, W$_{\rm (opt)}$ = 50.093 $\mu$m and N$_{\rm (opt)}$ = 2.49×$10^{16}$ $cm^{-3}$.
elements.sent-2411-047: Fig. \ref{OPT1 (d) shows the relationship between R$_{\rm on,sp (opt)}$ and BV through Taylor method, Bessel method and simulation.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The consistency of the three results indicates the high accuracy of above optimization using Taylor method.

elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{fig4.png} \caption{E-field comparison of 4 structures along breakdown path ($E_{1}\begin{equation}F_{1}$ for 3DH$k$case1 and 2DH$k$, $A_{2}\end{equation}B_{2}$ for 3DH$k$case2, $E$$F$ for 3D C-SJ), with 3D depiction of entire E-field of 3D C-SJ. } \label{efield}
elements.sent-2411-049: The structure of 2DH$k$ is the same as the profile of 3DH$k$case1.
elements.sent-2411-050: For 3D C-SJ, AR$_{\rm S}$ = W/ [2×(Width of P region)] and ensure charge neutrality for 3D C-SJ in all subsequent discussions.
elements.sent-2411-051: It is obvious that, from the figure, 3D C-SJ has the highest peak E-field and 3DH$k$case1 has the lowest, suggesting that the 3D C-SJ is more prone to breakdown under the same conditions, while the 3DH$k$case1 is less likely to break down under the same conditions.
elements.sent-2411-052: Using equation (10) and methodology in \cite{EFL with parameters set as follows\cite{hhm202203 : W = 63.3 $\mu$m, the width of the P region (H$k$ region) = 6.33 $\mu$m, the width of the N region = 2.62 $\mu$m, N = 1.97×10$^{15}$ cm$^{-3}$, $V_{\rm ap}$ = 900 V, K = 50.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: The values of the impact ionization integral along specific paths are shown in Fig. \ref{ion .}
elements.sent-2411-054: From the figure, it can be seen that consistent with the conclusion of Fig. \ref{efield , the 3D C-SJ has the largest impact ionization integral value at point F, meaning it is more likely to occur avalanche break down under the same conditions, while the 3DH$k$case1 is the least likely to occur.}

elements.sent-2411-055: The optimization trends of the four structures are shown in Fig. \ref{OPT2 (a), (b), and (c).}
elements.sent-2411-056: The optimized design parameters acquired from Fig. \ref{OPT2 (a) are shown in TABLE I.}
elements.sent-2411-057: For the 3DH$k$case2, the optimization results and trends are similar to those in Fig. \ref{OPT1 .}
elements.sent-2411-058: Additionally, the optimization results of the Taylor method and the Bessel method exhibit a high degree of consistency.
elements.sent-2411-059: It can be observed from Fig. \ref{OPT2 (a) and TABLE I that, except for the 3DH$k$case2, the remaining three structures all have a minimum value for R$_{\rm on,sp (opt)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-060: For the 3DH$k$case1, the minimum value (12.427) is achieved at a small AR$_{\rm S}$, and its minimum value is higher than that of 3D C-SJ, 2DH$k$ and 3DH$k$case2 by 72\%, 43\% and 55\%, implying that the 3DH$k$case1 structure is challenging to reduce R$_{\rm on,sp (opt)}$ under the given BV.
elements.sent-2411-061: It is noteworthy that the 3D C-SJ can optimize a smallest R$_{\rm on,sp (opt)}$ (3.488) at a larger AR$_{\rm S}$ compared to the other structures, which means that the 3D C-SJ can achieve a more favorable R$_{\rm on,sp (opt)}$ under the given BV compared to other structures.
elements.sent-2411-062: For the 2DH$k$, the minimum value is relatively less pronounced, however, its smallest R$_{\rm on,sp (opt)}$ (7.104) lies between that of the 3DH$k$case2 (5.653) and the 3DH$k$case1 (12.427).
elements.sent-2411-063: The minimum R$_{\rm on,sp (opt)}$ for the 3DH$k$case2 is still achieved at the maximum AR$_{\rm S}$, with its smallest R$_{\rm on,sp (opt)}$ (5.653) achieve 120\% and 26\% better than that of the 3DH$k$case1 and 2DH$k$, but 38\% worse than that of 3D C-SJ.
elements.sent-2411-064: Taking Fig. \ref{3DOPT (a) as an example, for larger AR$_{\rm S}$ and smaller BV, 2DH$k$ can achieve a smaller R$_{\rm on,sp (opt)}$, while for smaller AR$_{\rm S}$ and larger BV, 3DH$k$case1 can achieve a smaller R$_{\rm on,sp (opt)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Quantitatively speaking, if the AR$_{\rm S}$ that can be achieved in device production is determined, the critical BV (c-BV) can be obtained through the boundary curve formula shown in Fig. \ref{3DOPT (a).}
elements.sent-2411-066: If a device which requires a BV greater than the c-BV needs to be produced, 3DH$k$case1 is the better choice because of lower R$_{\rm on,sp (opt)}$.
elements.sent-2411-067: If the BV of the device is determined, the critical AR$_{\rm S}$ (c-AR$_{\rm S}$) can be obtained through the boundary curve formula.
elements.sent-2411-068: If the production process supports the creation of a large AR$_{\rm S}$, i.e., AR$_{\rm S}$ $>$ c-AR$_{\rm S}$, 2DH$k$ is the better choice.
elements.sent-2411-069: The same principle applies to Fig. \ref{3DOPT (b) and (c).}
elements.sent-2411-070: By using the formulas of the boundary curves, we can compare the advantages and disadvantages of 2DH$k$, 3DH$k$case1 and 3DH$k$case2 under given conditions, thereby providing valuable guidance for the production of 3D H$k$-SJ devices.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{imbalance.png} \caption{Charge imbalance effect of BV and R$_{\rm on,sp (opt)}$ as a function of deviation of N for the four different structures at condition 1 and 2.} \label{chargeim}
elements.figure-2411-011: \subfigure[]{\includegraphics[width=0.4\linewidth]{figTa.png} } \subfigure[]{\includegraphics[width=0.4\linewidth]{figTb.png} } \caption{R$_{\rm on,sp}$ as a function of temperature for three 3D H$k$-SJ structures at (a) different N and (b) different AR$_{\rm S}$. } \label{temp}
elements.sent-2411-071: Fig. \ref{chargeim illustrates the degradation of BV and R$_{\rm on,sp (opt)}$ due to the error in N.}
elements.sent-2411-072: The structural parameter settings are divided into two conditions.
elements.sent-2411-073: The first condition (solid lines) is consistent with Fig. \ref{ion , while the second condition (dashed lines) modifies the width of the P region (H$k$ region) to 3.3 $\mu$m and the width of the N region to 1.367 $\mu$m based on the first condition.}
elements.sent-2411-074: It can be observed from the Fig. \ref{chargeim that regardless of the structure parameters, the sensitivity of BV to errors in all high-$k$ superjunction structures is far less than that of the 3D C-SJ.}
elements.sent-2411-075: This implies that the use of various high-$k$ superjunction structures can significantly enhance the robustness of BV against N errors.
elements.sent-2411-076: Regarding R$_{\rm on,sp (opt)}$, all H$k$-SJs are noticeably affected by errors, but 3DH$k$case1 is more sensitive to errors compared to 2DH$k$ and 3DH$k$case2.
elements.sent-2411-077: From Fig. \ref{temp (a), it is evident that 3DH$k$case1's R$_{\rm on,sp (opt)}$ is the most sensitive to temperature, while the R$_{\rm on,sp (opt)}$ of 3DH$k$case2 and 2DH$k$ exhibit similar robustness against temperature variations.}
elements.sent-2411-078: This implies that if the device is expected to operate under high-temperature conditions, 3DH$k$case2 and 2DH$k$ are the better choices.
elements.sent-2411-079: Additionally, appropriately increasing the $N$ can reduce the temperature sensitivity of 3DH$k$case1.
elements.sent-2411-080: As shown in Fig. \ref{temp (a), a higher doping concentration will decrease the exponent of T from 2.2003 to 2.1516.}

elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{output.png} \caption{Output characteristic of three 3D H$k$-SJ MOSFETs by simulation results at different $V_{\rm GS}$.} \label{output}
elements.section-2411-004: Electrical Characteristic Analysis of different H$k$-SJ MOSFETs
elements.sent-2411-081: Here, W=75 $\mu$m, N=5×10$^{15}$ cm$^{-3}$, N region width=2 $\mu$m, H$k$ region width=6 $\mu$m, K=20, and the doping concentrations for the Drain region, Substrate, and Source region are 1×10$^{19}$, 2×10$^{17}$, and 3×10$^{19}$ cm$^{-3}$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-082: The channel length is approximately 4 $\mu$m, and the gate oxide thickness is 35 nm.
elements.sent-2411-083: CONSRH, AUGER, ANALYTIC, FLDMOB, BGN, INCOMPLE and IMPACT.I are used as physical models in MEDICI simulation.
elements.sent-2411-084: Fig. \ref{output demonstrates the static output characteristics and breakdown behavior of three different H$k$-SJ MOSFETs, where $j_{\rm D}$ is the current density of the drain and $V_{\rm DS}$ is the applied voltage across the drain and source.}
elements.sent-2411-085: As shown in Fig. \ref{efield and Fig. \ref{ion , 3DH$k$case1, with its smaller peak breakdown electric field and impact ionization integral value, is the most difficult one to break down among the three structures at $V_{\rm GS}$ = 0 V, followed by 2DH$k$, while 3DH$k$case2 is the easiest one to break down.}}
elements.figure-2411-014: \subfigure[]{\includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{swon.png} } \subfigure[]{ \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{swoff.png} } \caption{ Switching responses of three 3D H$k$-SJ MOSFETs by simulation results. (a) Switch-on. (b) Switch-off} \label{sw}
elements.sent-2411-086: Fig. \ref{sw (a) and (b) display the switching responses of the three structures.}
elements.sent-2411-087: Here, $V_{\rm GS}$ is ramped up from 0 V to 4 V (and down from 4 V to 0 V) within 0.1 ns, while $V_{\rm dd}$ is maintained at 200 V.
elements.sent-2411-088: From Fig. \ref{sw (a), it can be observed that 2DH$k$ turns on the fastest, followed by 3DH$k$case1, and then 3DH$k$case2.}
elements.sent-2411-089: From Fig. \ref{sw (b), it is evident that 3DH$k$case2 turns off the quickest, followed by 2DH$k$, and then 3DH$k$case1.}
elements.sent-2411-090: This implies that 3DH$k$case1 has relatively poor switching characteristics, while 3DH$k$case2 is suitable for applications in fast chopper circuits.
elements.sent-2411-091: Through comparative analysis under the same conditions among 3D C-SJ, 2DH$k$, 3DH$k$case1 and 3DH$k$case2, we find that 3D C-SJ has the potential to optimize for the smallest R$_{\rm on,sp}$ to 3.488 $m\Omega \cdot cm^{2}$ at BV = 800 V but is the worst at resisting N deviation.
elements.sent-2411-092: The performance of 2DH$k$ is usually between case1 and case2.
elements.sent-2411-093: Furthermore, through the formulas of boundary curves, we can provide effective guidance for structure selection in superjunction device manufacturing.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
